description: The provided image is an irrelevant software interface icon, composed of a red bookmark-like graphic inside a circular ring with upper blue and lower yellow segments, and the text "Check for updates" printed below the graphic. This icon has no correlation with the target paper's research on the microstructure and corrosion behavior of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steels with 0 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt% Cu addition. No valid material characterization data, performance test results, mechanism schematics or other research-related information can be extracted from this image, indicating an image upload or loading error.

This figure contains three scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs and one X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern for Fe-30Mn-8Al-C low-density steels with 0 wt%, 1 wt%, and 3 wt% Cu addition (denoted as 0Cu, 1Cu, 3Cu respectively). Subfigures (a), (b), (c) show the surface microstructure of 0Cu, 1Cu, 3Cu samples separately, each with a 10 μm scale bar. All samples exhibit typical equiaxed γ-austenite grains, with white precipitates distributed in different positions: 0Cu has a small quantity of precipitates inside grains; 1Cu has denser intragranular precipitates plus a small amount of precipitates at grain boundaries; 3Cu presents obvious enrichment of white Cu-rich precipitates at grain boundaries with fewer intragranular precipitates. Subfigure (d) gives XRD patterns of the three samples, all only showing characteristic γ-austenite diffraction peaks ((111), (200), (220), (311)) without detectable second phase peaks, verifying single austenite matrix for all samples. The figure proves that Cu addition does not change the matrix phase composition of the steel, but significantly adjusts the distribution of precipitates, providing solid microstructure basis for the following corrosion behavior analysis.

This figure contains 6 subplots, focusing on grain structure and size statistics of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (a), 1 wt% Cu (b), 3 wt% Cu (c), all processed by vacuum melting, Ar atmosphere casting, 1200℃ homogenization for 2 h, multi-pass hot rolling above 850℃, and air cooling to room temperature. Subplots (a)-(c) are color-dyed optical micrographs with 20 μm scale bars, showing equiaxed austenite grains, with measured ASTM grain size numbers of 10.2, 9.5, 9.6 respectively. The other 3 subplots are grain equivalent diameter distribution histograms with fitted distribution curves, with x-axis as equivalent diameter (μm) and y-axis as count. As ASTM grain size number is inversely proportional to actual grain size, Cu-containing samples have larger austenite grains than the Cu-free sample, while 1Cu and 3Cu samples have comparable grain sizes. This figure supports the conclusion that 0.5-3 wt% Cu addition promotes austenite grain nucleation and growth, increasing average austenite grain size.

This figure shows SEM micrographs and EPMA elemental mapping results of Fe-30Mn-8Al-C low-density steels with 1 wt% Cu (group a) and 3 wt% Cu (group b), respectively, with a 10 μm scale bar for SEM images. For the 1Cu sample, Al and Mo are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix; the bright white particles in the SEM image marked by red boxes match the co-enrichment positions of Nb and C, which are identified as NbC-type carbides, and slight Cu segregation is detected at austenite grain boundaries. For the 3Cu sample, Al exhibits uneven distribution at partial grain boundaries, and obvious strong Cu enrichment appears at grain boundaries, demonstrating that part of the white particles at grain boundaries are elemental Cu, besides residual NbC carbides. The results clarify the influence of Cu content on second phase composition and elemental segregation behavior, providing direct microstructure evidence for the difference in corrosion performance of the tested steels.

This figure characterizes carbide precipitation characteristics of Fe-30Mn-8Al-C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (subfigure a), 1 wt% Cu (subfigure b), and 3 wt% Cu (subfigure c). Subfigures a-c are optical metallographic micrographs with 20 μm scale bars, presenting equiaxed austenite grains and dispersed carbide precipitates; the measured mean grain size numbers are 0.52, 0.51, 0.61, and total carbide counts are 1740, 2377, 876 for 0Cu, 1Cu, 3Cu samples respectively. The remaining three subfigures are histograms of carbide equivalent diameter distribution, with x-axis as equivalent diameter (μm) and y-axis as count. The results indicate that 1 wt% Cu addition refines carbides and increases their quantity, while 3 wt% Cu suppresses C diffusion, leading to sharply reduced carbide count and increased average size, which demonstrates Cu content significantly regulates carbide precipitation in the studied steel, correlating with its corrosion performance.

This figure presents three-electrode potentiodynamic polarization curves for hot-rolled Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steels with varying Cu additions: 0 wt% Cu (0Cu, black line with square markers), 1 wt% Cu (1Cu, red line with cross markers), and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu, blue line with triangle markers), measured in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to evaluate corrosion performance. The x-axis is log₁₀ of current density (log i, unit: A/cm²) spanning 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻³ A/cm², while the y-axis is electrochemical potential referenced to saturated calomel electrode (E vs SCE, unit: V) ranging from -0.5 V to -0.2 V. With increasing Cu content, corrosion potential shifts positively, pitting potential shifts negatively, and passivation current density decreases. 0Cu exhibits the highest pitting potential (~-0.215 V vs SCE) and lowest corrosion rate, while 3Cu shows the lowest pitting potential (~-0.245 V vs SCE) and highest corrosion rate. The results confirm that Cu alloying reduces the corrosion resistance of this low-density steel system, with resistance declining as Cu content rises from 0 to 3 wt%, consistent with microgalvanic corrosion induced by grain boundary Cu enrichment and carbide precipitates.

This figure presents Mott-Schottky curves of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C low-density steel samples with different Cu contents: 0 wt% Cu (0Cu, pink square line), 1 wt% Cu (1Cu, blue circle line), and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu, green triangle line). The x-axis represents potential versus saturated calomel electrode (V_SCE) ranging from -1.0 V to 1.0 V, and the y-axis represents C⁻² in units of 10⁹ F²·cm⁻⁴. Three potential regions (I, II, III) are divided. The peak value of C⁻² decreases gradually as Cu content increases, while the corresponding peak potential shifts slightly, indicating that carrier density in the passive film increases with higher Cu addition. These results align with the trend that corrosion resistance of the steel reduces with increased Cu content, supporting the conclusion that Cu alloying deteriorates the corrosion performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steels with 0, 1, 3 wt% Cu addition (denoted 0 Cu, 1 Cu, 3 Cu respectively) tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subplot (a) is the Nyquist diagram, plotting imaginary impedance Zim against real impedance Zre, with an inset equivalent circuit (solution resistance Rs in series with a parallel combination of charge transfer resistance Rct and constant phase element CPE) used for data fitting. Subplot (b) is the Bode diagram, showing impedance modulus |Z| and negative phase angle as functions of frequency from 1E-3 Hz to 1E6 Hz. All experimental data points are well matched by fitting curves. The diameter of the capacitive semicircle in the Nyquist plot decreases with increasing Cu content: 0 Cu has the largest semicircle (highest charge transfer resistance), followed by 1 Cu and 3 Cu. The Bode plot also shows decreasing |Z| and peak phase angle with higher Cu content. These results confirm that Cu alloying deteriorates the corrosion resistance of the studied low-density steel, and higher Cu content leads to worse corrosion performance.

This figure presents optical micrographs of corrosion morphology for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (a₁ to a₅), 1 wt% Cu (b₁ to b₅), and 3 wt% Cu (c₁ to c₅) after 1 to 5 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, respectively, with a uniform 20 μm scale bar for all subfigures. The 0Cu series shows isolated, gradually growing pitting corrosion pits with limited cross-matrix propagation as immersion time increases. The 1Cu series exhibits connected, chain-like corrosion regions that expand significantly over 5 days, indicating accelerated pit propagation. The 3Cu series shows pitting initiation preferentially near grain boundaries visible as equiaxed grain networks in c subfigures, with corrosion damage worsening over time. Morphology evolution confirms higher Cu content degrades alloy corrosion resistance, consistent with measured corrosion rates, supporting that Cu grain boundary segregation and microgalvanic effects accelerate corrosion in Cu-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This group of figures presents the immersion corrosion test results of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steels with different Cu contents (0, 1, 3 wt%, denoted as 0Cu, 1Cu, 3Cu respectively). Subplot (a) is the weight loss curve over 5 days of immersion, with y-axis as weight loss (g) and x-axis as immersion time (d). The weight loss of all samples increases with immersion time, and higher Cu content leads to larger weight loss, reaching ~0.002 g for 0Cu, ~0.003 g for 1Cu and ~0.0034 g for 3Cu at 5 d. Subplot (b) is the bar chart of average corrosion rate (unit: mm·a⁻¹) for the three samples. Corrosion rate rises with increasing Cu content, with 0Cu showing the lowest rate of 0.0546 mm/a, 1Cu ~0.081 mm/a, 3Cu ~0.092 mm/a. The figure supports that Cu alloying deteriorates the corrosion resistance of the tested low-density steel, and the corrosion severity increases with Cu content, consistent with previous electrochemical test results.

This figure presents scanning Kelvin probe force microscopy (SKPFM, an atomic force microscopy variant) characterization results for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (0Cu) and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu). Subfigures (a) and (d) are surface height maps for 0Cu (height range -1.9 μm to 802.5 nm, 4 μm scale bar) and 3Cu (height range -375.3 nm to 832.5 nm, 4 μm scale bar), respectively. Subfigures (b) and (e) are corresponding Volta potential maps for 0Cu (potential range -5.8 mV to 13 mV) and 3Cu (potential range -9.4 mV to 18.2 mV), respectively. Subfigures (c) and (f) are Volta potential line profiles along the red lines in (b) and (e), showing measured maximum potential differences of ~0.9 mV for 0Cu and ~1.2 mV for 3Cu. The results confirm that the austenite matrix has lower Volta potential than carbide precipitates and Cu-enriched grain boundaries, verifying austenite acts as the anodic phase prone to preferential dissolution via microgalvanic corrosion. Increased Cu content elevates the Volta potential difference, especially at grain boundaries due to Cu segregation, explaining the degraded corrosion resistance of higher-Cu steels, supporting the core conclusion that Cu alloying aggravates microgalvanic corrosion in this low-density steel system.

This set of figures presents TEM characterization results of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-0Cu (0Cu) low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field TEM image of the austenitic matrix with a 2 μm scale bar. Subfigure (b) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of intragranular precipitates along the [001] zone axis, with diffraction spots indexed as 002, 0-20, 00-1, and 01-1. Subfigure (c) is the dark-field morphology of intragranular precipitates with a 20 nm scale bar, revealing nanoscale dispersed precipitates. Subfigure (d) displays the carbide morphology with a 200 nm scale bar. Subfigure (e) is a high-resolution TEM image of carbides with a 10 nm scale bar, accompanied by its SAED pattern also along the [001] zone axis. Subfigure (f) shows the inverse fast Fourier transform (FFT) image near the carbide with a 10 nm scale bar, identifying dislocations at the carbide-matrix interface. These results confirm the presence of nanoscale intragranular κ-carbides and micro-sized NbC carbides in the 0Cu austenite matrix, as well as lattice distortion and dislocations near carbide interfaces, providing direct microstructural evidence for the corrosion mechanism that high strain energy dislocation cores act as anodic sites to initiate pitting corrosion in 0Cu steel.

This figure presents TEM characterization results of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel with 1 wt% Cu addition (1Cu sample). Subfigure (a) shows the morphology of the austenitic matrix with a scale bar of 1 μm. Subfigure (b) is the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of intracrystalline precipitates with zone axis Z=[001] (scale bar 5 1/nm), with diffraction spots of the matrix and precipitates indexed. Subfigure (c) displays the dark-field morphology of nanoscale intracrystalline precipitates, with a scale bar of 20 nm. Subfigure (d) shows the morphology of micron-sized carbide particles, with a scale bar of 200 nm. Subfigure (e) provides the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the carbide and its corresponding SAED pattern (zone axis Z=[001]), with a scale bar of 10 nm. Subfigure (f) shows the inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) image near the carbide-matrix interface (scale bar 10 nm), revealing lattice distortion and dislocations at the interface. These results confirm the microstructure features of austenite matrix, nanoscale precipitates and carbides in 1Cu steel, supporting analysis of Cu alloying effects on the microstructure evolution of this low-density steel system.

This figure consists of 8 subplots with a uniform 20 nm scale bar, acquired from Cu-alloyed Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel: the first is a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph showing a grain boundary as a distinct dark linear feature, and the remaining 7 are energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps for Cu, Al, C, Cr, Fe, Nb, and Mo respectively. In the Cu elemental map, two yellow rectangular markers explicitly highlight regions of Cu particle aggregation at the grain boundary, while all other measured elements (Al, C, Cr, Fe, Nb, Mo) exhibit homogeneous distribution across the entire analyzed area without observable segregation. These correlated TEM and EDS results directly confirm the preferential aggregation of Cu particles at grain boundaries in Cu-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, which supports the corrosion mechanism that grain boundary Cu aggregation induces microgalvanic corrosion and reduces the alloy's corrosion resistance as Cu content increases.

This figure presents high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of corrosion products on Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (0Cu), 1 wt% Cu (1Cu), and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu) after 5 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subplots cover Fe 2p3/2, Mn 2p3/2, Al 2p, O 1s, and Cu 2p3/2 core levels. Fe 2p3/2 is deconvoluted into Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃, and FeOOH; Mn 2p3/2 into MnO and MnO₂; Al 2p only corresponds to Al₂O₃; O 1s is split into O²⁻, OH⁻, and C=O groups; Cu 2p3/2 (for Cu-containing samples) is fitted to Cu₂O and CuO. Quantitatively, Mn oxide content increases with rising Cu content, while Al₂O₃ content is highest in 0Cu. These results verify that Cu addition increases the proportion of electron-conductive Mn oxides in the passive film, accelerating corrosion, which aligns with the measured trend of decreasing corrosion resistance with increasing Cu content.

This stacked bar chart presents the relative surface oxide composition of three Fe-30Mn-8Al-C low-density steels with different Cu contents: 0 wt% Cu (0Cu), 1 wt% Cu (1Cu), and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu), calculated from XPS characterization results. The detected oxides include Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃, FeOOH, MnO, MnO₂, Al₂O₃, Cu₂O, and CuO. As Cu content increases, the content of corrosion-resistant Al₂O₃ decreases gradually from 38.2% (0Cu) to 28.2% (1Cu) and 16.3% (3Cu), while the total proportion of Mn oxides (MnO + MnO₂) increases from 47% (0Cu) to 57.2% (1Cu) and 77% (3Cu). The proportions of Fe-based oxides and Cu-based oxides are relatively low for all samples. The results reveal that Cu alloying reduces the content of protective Al₂O₃ and increases the proportion of Mn oxides that accelerate electron transfer, which explains the higher corrosion rate of Cu-containing steels, consistent with corresponding electrochemical test results.

This figure presents X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of passive films formed on Fe-30Mn-8Al-C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (subplots a1 to a4) and 3 wt% Cu (subplots b1 to b5), after potentiostatic polarization at -0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 7200 s. Fe 2p3/2 (a1,b1) spectra are fitted to Fe3O4, Fe2O3, and FeOOH; Mn 2p3/2 (a2,b2) to MnO and MnO2; Al 2p (a3,b3) to Al2O3 and metallic Al; O 1s (a4,b4) to C=O, OH⁻, and O²⁻; and Cu 2p3/2 (b5, only for 3Cu sample) to Cu2O and CuO, with labeled binding energies and quantified atomic percentages for each species. Key comparisons show the 0Cu sample has higher Al2O3 content (66.48% vs 60.75% for 3Cu), lower MnO (54.11% vs 63.84% for 3Cu) and lower OH⁻ (32.18% vs 38.37% for 3Cu) contents. The results confirm that Cu alloying reduces passive film stability, accelerating microgalvanic corrosion and decreasing the steel's corrosion resistance.

This figure is a stacked bar chart illustrating the oxide proportion of passivation films formed on Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cu (0Cu sample) and 3 wt% Cu (3Cu sample), after potentiostatic polarization treatment in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to obtain stable passivation films. The quantified phases in the passivation film include Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃, FeOOH, MnO, MnO₂, Al₂O₃, metallic Al, Cu₂O and CuO, with their respective mass percentages marked. For the 0Cu sample, the proportions are: Fe₃O₄ 4.8%, Fe₂O₃ 7.4%, FeOOH 5.9%, MnO 35.5%, MnO₂ 30%, Al₂O₃ 10.9%, Al 5.5%, with negligible Cu-containing oxide content. For the 3Cu sample, the proportions are: Fe₃O₄ 4.3%, Fe₂O₃ 6.7%, FeOOH 5.4%, MnO 43.5%, MnO₂ 24.6%, Al₂O₃ 8.1%, Al 5.3%, plus 1.1% Cu₂O and 1% CuO. Comparison shows that Cu addition increases MnO content, reduces protective Al₂O₃ and MnO₂ content, and introduces unstable Cu oxides, which explains the deteriorated passivation film stability and corrosion resistance of Cu-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, supporting the core conclusion that higher Cu content impairs the corrosion performance of the tested low-density steel.

This schematic diagram illustrates the corrosion mechanism of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C low-density steels with 0 wt%, 1 wt%, and 3 wt% Cu (marked as 0Cu, 1Cu, 3Cu) exposed to 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Left subpanels show early and late corrosion stages for each sample: 0Cu has sparse matrix carbides that initiate mild pitting at lattice distortion sites around carbides; 1Cu has Cu-refined dense dispersed carbides, which form multiple microgalvanic couples with the austenite matrix to generate more pitting sites; 3Cu contains undissolved elemental Cu enriched at grain boundaries alongside carbides, leading to synergistic microgalvanic corrosion that induces both intergranular corrosion and severe pitting. Right subpanels display the corresponding corroded surface morphologies for each sample. Four typical corrosion reaction equations for Fe, Al, Mn, and Cu during the corrosion process are also listed. This figure supports the core conclusion that increasing Cu addition deteriorates the corrosion resistance of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel, with 3Cu exhibiting the worst corrosion performance.
